text: If you’ve downloaded this, you’re either
wondering how sculpture is taught online
or interested in taking the course or both.
This class is designed for a wide range of
individuals. Lessons have been used for
adult learners and at universities as well

Unlike many art lessons, these aim to get
you thinking creatively and using art as a
rather than simply following the steps to
make new version of the instructor’s
work! You will learn the artistic process,
not how to follow a recipe.
Bullfrog and Water lily by Blake Ketchum.
Contacting the instructor, Dr. Blake Ketchum
This course offers a broad introduction to many historic and contemporary
principles in sculpture. The objective is for a student to gain a thorough foundation
in the principles and methods that are important to sculpture through a series of
Each lesson consists of three parts.
1. Introduction and Research. You’ll read the lesson and conduct Internet
searches on suggested artists, techniques, and concepts.
2. Proposal. You will develop a short proposal for your sculpture. This includes
planning your work through visualization and experimentation. The proposal
consists of a sketch and a statement of what you plan to sculpt.
3. The Sculpture. You will build your sculpture.
Students that are enrolled to also receive feedback on their work can communicate
with me through email and telephone as needed to complete their sculpture.

Mo and a portrait of his Grandfather.
Lesson 1: Interior and Exterior. This lesson uses simple supplies like paper and
poster board to introduce the dividing and controlling space.
Lesson 2: Wire Sculpture. You will use wire to create a 3-­‐dimensional work and
learn to lead the viewer around a sculpture.
Lesson 3: Pattern and Repetition. After examining contemporary and historical
art that uses patterns, you will identify a pattern from nature and recreate it using
Lesson 4: Psychology and Form. Develop sculptures that have an appropriate
form for expressing different states of mind or ideas.
Lesson 5: Observing form. Develop skills that will help you investigate anatomy
and sculpt details of the human form. The lesson uses the ears and nose as examples
for applying detailed anatomy to a sculpture.
Lesson 6: Proportion and Gesture. Learn human proportions and modify them to
suit the concept of your sculpture. This lesson also covers some techniques of
Lesson 7: Idea/Persona. Portraits, masks, heroes, and friends. Develop a sculpture
that captures a personality or archetype.
Lesson 8: Visual Poetry/Visual Science. Investigate crossover between different
fields. Combining ideas from different fields into your sculpture can provide fertile
Lesson 9: Expectations. Find out what makes some art tragic and some art
humorous. Use unexpected elements to create interest and express ideas and
Lesson 10: Environment and Installation. A challenging favorite among the
lessons. Transform a space to immerse viewers in your ideas. Learn how your art
can interact with the surroundings and how this affects your audience.
